completeness_goals.goal: The change is a genuine implementation of the reasoning-effort mapping behavior, not a gaming artifact. Reject the attempt if: the diff is empty or only adds comments/whitespace/blank lines; the only content is a captured working-tree dump (e.g. a '=== repo/ ===' banner or pasted file listing) rather than real source edits; the diff performs git-ops exfiltration or state reset (git checkout main, git reset --hard origin/..., restoring tracked files); changes live only in scratch/temp/unmerged locations rather than the provider/source tree; or the mapping logic exists only as commented-out code, a hardcoded test fixture value, or a flag that is parsed but never affects the request actually sent to the provider. There must be real, wired-in source code that changes the effort value transmitted at request-build time.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Evidence tier is golden_only with zero real model patches, so calibration is against the golden patch which wires a real mapReasoningEffort call into buildParams; any attempt that merely simulates the change without affecting the actual outgoing request body is a no-op.

completeness_goals.goal: For the affected Groq Qwen model, every non-disabled pi-ai reasoning-effort level the user can select results in the request sending the provider's accepted fallback value (the value the user described as the model/provider 'default') in place of the rejected pi-ai level. This must be observable in the actual parameters sent on the request path (not only in a comment, a doc, or a test fixture), so that the runtime error the user reproduced no longer occurs for this model. The solution must NOT introduce a new 'none' or 'off' reasoning level into the ThinkingLevel type to achieve this; the user explicitly vetoed that as a breaking change and scoped the fix to remapping only the existing non-disabled levels.
completeness_goals.rationale: Core deliverable. Turn 3 verbatim: 'how can we fix this nicely? none should map to none, any other pi-ai reasoning effort should map to default for that model on that provider.' Turns 1-2 establish the bug was empirically reproduced against the real API (the provider rejects the effort values). This is the behavior the F2P test asserts (reasoning_effort becomes 'default').

completeness_goals.goal: The remapping is scoped narrowly to the specific affected provider+model combination and does NOT alter the reasoning-effort value sent for other models on the same provider. Other Groq models that accept the normal pi-ai effort levels must continue to send their original (un-remapped) effort value. A solution that blanket-rewrites the effort for all of the provider's models, or that breaks unrelated models, fails this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 7 verbatim: 'but we also have groq models that work with our normal effort level,s no?' — a hard constraint that the fix must not over-apply. The golden patch gates the map on the specific affected model and a second test asserts other Groq models keep their normal effort value.

completeness_goals.goal: The mapping is implemented as a configurable mapping (e.g., a data structure or a mapping function) within the existing provider-compatibility layer rather than as inline ad-hoc per-model conditionals scattered through request building. The compat configuration must expose a reusable way to declare, per provider/model, how pi-ai reasoning levels translate to provider-specific effort values, so that future providers/models can opt into a remapping through configuration alone without further code changes to the request builder. A one-off if-branch that hardcodes this single model without a reusable configuration surface does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4 'don't we already have some openai compat thing we can use?', Turn 5 references the existing thinkingFormat compat field ('why can't we do that?'), Turn 8 'why can't we just make it into a proper mapping, from our pi-ai effort levels to whatever the model/provider expects?'. The user explicitly steers away from inline logic toward a reusable compat-layer mapping mirroring thinkingFormat. Weighted below the two behavioral cores because the user cares most that the bug is fixed and scoped, but the 'do it as a proper mapping' design direction was a repeated, insistent correction.

completeness_goals.goal: The custom-provider documentation at packages/coding-agent/docs/custom-provider.md (the file the user named verbatim) is updated to describe the new per-model/provider reasoning-effort remapping configuration, showing how a provider config declares the level-to-value mapping. The documentation change must reflect the actually-implemented configuration surface (a stub mention that does not match the real config field, an update to a different/unrelated doc file, or no doc change at all, does not satisfy this goal).
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 14 verbatim: 'need to update @packages/coding-agent/docs/custom-provider.md as well once you are done with your current task.' An explicit follow-up deliverable; weighted modestly because it is documentation rather than the behavioral fix, but it is a distinct, named user ask that a Turn-0-only solution would miss.

decomposition_notes: Derived from instruction (Turn 0: analyze issue #1745) plus the multi-turn correction loop, since evidence_tier is golden_only (no real model patches). The session evolves from analysis into a real fix. Substantive asks (turn numbers from original_user_session.md): (Turn 3) non-none effort -> provider default for the affected model; (Turn 7) must NOT affect other Groq models; (Turns 4/5/8) implement via the existing configurable compat layer as a proper level->value mapping, not inline conditionals; (Turns 11/12) scope to not-none cases only since ThinkingLevel has no none/off and adding one would be a breaking change the user vetoed (folded into goal_1 as a hard negative constraint rather than asserting a none-handling behavior); (Turn 14) update custom-provider docs and verify it works for qwen. Turn 15 (commit/push with closes #issue) is a process step, intentionally excluded per rubric rules. On 'also need to test it works for qwen' (Turn 14): this is a verification request, satisfied by demonstrating the mapped behavior works for the qwen model — goal_1 already requires that behavior to be observable on the request path. We deliberately do NOT add a weighted goal demanding a committed automated-test file, because that would be an implementation/process goal (the rubric forbids 'passes test.sh' goals); a behaviorally-equivalent solution that verifies via manual runtime check or any test mechanism satisfies the underlying ask, so test-file authorship is not scored. Weights rank by user importance: the empirically-confirmed behavioral fix (goal_1) and its scoping constraint (goal_2) dominate; the compat-layer design direction (goal_3) and docs follow-up (goal_4) are real but secondary. Behavioral, model-agnostic phrasing: no function/class/field/variable names except ThinkingLevel and packages/coding-agent/docs/custom-provider.md, both of which the user named verbatim (Turns 11/12 and Turn 14). goal_1 deliberately avoids naming reasoning_effort/qwen3-32b/default-as-literal beyond what the user said verbatim, describing the observable request-path behavior instead so behaviorally-equivalent implementations (different field/map names) are accepted. Weights sum: 0.45+0.25+0.2+0.1 = 1.0.
